reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_190652_bf16_gemm_v1_88a8acfc recovered the accepted compact PTX cadence and only improved to 24.689153 ms, which is effectively a local plateau on this branch: the same 22,016 B shared surface still carries 201 regs/thread, 16.61% active warps, 48.39% tensor activity, 8.14% barrier, and 5.16% long-scoreboard. The last two rounds showed the current 128x128 PTX loop can still trade tiny gains by undoing regressions, but it is not breaking past the 24.69 ms shelf. That shifts the ranking toward structural directions that can change the occupancy/reuse geometry without replaying the stale restore-base family. I rank the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64-warp reopen first because it aligns with persistent human guidance, is already present in source, and is the clearest way to test whether the remaining gap is geometric rather than just loop cadence. I rank the 2-K-stage Pg2s PTX port second because the in-tree 128x128x32 schedule shows a concrete way to amortize waits and reloads while staying on the 128x128 dispatch surface. I keep a pairwise wait/sync-collapse re-open third as the higher-risk hot-loop control experiment for the current PTX family once the two broader ceilings are sampled.
family_audit: accept: the compact two-stage PTX 128x128 path with grouped_rows=4 remains the current accepted base and should only be left for bounded structural experiments, not stale restore-base replays.
promote: elevate the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64-warp hot-band branch because the live 128x128 PTX surface is occupancy-capped at 2 resident CTAs and appears locally exhausted near 24.69 ms.

keep_open: retain the 2-K-stage Pg2s and pairwise wait/sync-collapse families as distinct ways to change feed cadence on the hot-band surface without changing the fixed benchmark shape or abandoning async-copy reuse.

directions.name: Reopen The Writer-Safe 256x128 64x64-Warp Hot-Band Branch From The Current Compact PTX Base
directions.hypothesis: The current 128x128 PTX surface is no longer obviously broken, but it is still capped by a 201-register, 2-CTA occupancy ceiling and only 16.61% active warps. Reopening the correctness-safe 256x128 / 64x64-warp branch from the current compact PTX base is the cleanest way to test whether the remaining gap is geometric and reuse-related rather than another tiny wait/sync tweak.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The dominant ceiling is occupancy and hot-band geometry on the active 128x128 PTX path, not raw DRAM bandwidth.
directions.risk: Moderate-high. This is a structural reopen that changes hot-band CTA geometry and export scheduling, but it stays on the writer-safe path already present in source.
ranking_notes: The compact PTX branch has spent multiple rounds recovering regressions and is still parked around 24.69 ms, so another pure local exploit is less attractive than a geometry branch.
This candidate directly matches the persistent human guidance to prefer a 256x128 CTA tile with a 64x64 warp tile.
The writer-safe implementation already exists in-tree, so the reopen is bounded and auditable rather than a speculative rewrite.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen requires reviving a previously incorrect writer/export path instead of the existing writer-safe 256x128 surface, or if registers blow up enough to reduce residency below the current effective limit.
expected_local_changes: Switch the fixed hot-band dispatch from the compact 128x128 PTX microkernel to the existing writer-safe 256x128 kernel.
Keep the peeled 64x384 row band and 64x96 tail unchanged on the first measurement.
Preserve async-copy staging and the right-left-right-left warp ownership path already embedded in the 256x128 kernel.

directions.name: Port The 2-K-Stage Pg2s Schedule Onto The Active 128x128 PTX Microkernel
directions.hypothesis: The compact PTX loop still pays one wait and one CTA barrier per K16 tile while long-scoreboard remains 5.16%. Porting the in-tree 128x128x32 two-K-stage schedule onto the PTX microkernel can amortize those waits and shared reloads over two K tiles at a time without reopening the full 256x128 geometry branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The active PTX hot-band path is still limited by per-tile Pg2s cadence and the resulting latency-hiding gap.
directions.risk: Moderate. The x32 staged schedule already exists, but porting its two-K-stage pipeline onto the PTX microkernel changes shared staging, wait-group cadence, and loop structure together.
ranking_notes: This is the best non-tiling structural follow-up because it reuses a proven in-tree schedule instead of inventing a new pipeline from scratch.
It targets both barrier and long-scoreboard together, which is a better fit for the current NCU picture than another tiny pointer or launch-order tweak.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the branch has already plateaued on the compact 128x128 geometry and the 256x128 guidance remains untested on the current recovered base.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the port requires a third shared stage, pushes static shared memory beyond the current budget without a clear latency-hiding benefit, or introduces a correctness-only control path that diverges from the x32 reference schedule.
expected_local_changes: Mirror the 128x128x32 kernel's two-K-stage preload and drain structure inside the PTX microkernel path.
Keep the 128x128 CTA geometry and grouped_rows=4 launch order unchanged in the first pass.
Reuse the hoisted shared-tile pointer setup already present in the active PTX microkernel.

directions.name: Reopen Pairwise Wait-Sync Collapse On The Current Compact PTX Surface
directions.hypothesis: Even after restoring the accepted cadence, the PTX hot-band loop still serializes on a wait-group plus CTA sync seam every K16 tile. Reopening a pairwise stage-advance collapse from the current compact base may cut barrier and scoreboard together, but it is riskier now because recent local timing work already showed how fragile the steady-state PTX control flow is.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The remaining bottleneck on the compact PTX surface is hot-loop synchronization cadence rather than address setup.
directions.risk: High. This directly edits the hottest PTX control path and overlaps conceptually with earlier failed drain and cadence experiments.
ranking_notes: This family still targets a real local issue, but it now ranks behind the geometry and 2-K-stage branches because the compact PTX surface has already burned multiple rounds on sync-sensitive fixes.
A historical win exists for wait/sync collapse on another base, so it remains a valid frontier family rather than dead code.
It should only be taken after the broader ceilings are sampled or if both higher-ranked structural probes fail cleanly.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the collapse requires extra predicates in the hot loop, produces any correctness mismatch, or lowers tensor active while barrier improvement stays within noise.
expected_local_changes: Collapse the per-tile wait/sync cadence into a pairwise stage advance on the active compact PTX loop.
Keep grouped_rows=4 and the current 22,016 B shared-memory surface unchanged in the first pass.
Avoid mixing this experiment with a simultaneous geometry or export rewrite.

notes: Round 11/20 diagnosis emitted from the live 24.689153 ms compact PTX plateau; frontier should prefer a structural branch over stale restore-base replay.
